summary:
The condyle portion of the mandibular ramus differentiates from Meckel's cartilage during fetal development before transitioning into a bony structure through endochondral ossification. This developmental pathway distinguishes it among other parts of the mandible. To summarize Agent2's reasoning for your query about what part of the mandible first forms via cartilage, here are two concise statements capturing essential details without extraneous content or repetition:

1) During early human development, specifically within Meckel's cartilage at approximately week ten after conception, only the condylar processes initiate their formation by being primarily chondrogenic rather than directly osteogenic.
   
2) Subsequently these regions convert entirely over time using an endochondrial mechanism typical throughout skeletogenesis but uniquely applied just there amongst all components constituting adult lower jawbone architecture." 

The summary focuses solely on conveying core facts pertinent to answering "What part?" while keeping sentence count low per instruction. Let me know if you need any clarification!
